files.content: # AxtraxNG.com — Site Reference Notes (Expanded) Source: https://axtraxng.com (Rosslare Security North American Technical Support portal) Captured: 2026-04-21 --- ## 1. Key Notices - **AxtraxNG End of Life: 12/31/2026** — software will not be available for download after this date. - **AxtraxNG Support ends 12/31/2027.** - Rosslare recommends using **AxtraxPro** for all new systems starting immediately. - Technical support registration is now **REQUIRED** via the Zoho form on the landing page. --- ## 2. Software — Current Releases ### AxtraxNG (legacy, not recommended for new installs) - Current / final version: **AxtraxNG 27.7.1.20** (release notes dated 2023-09-28, author Avi Eliav). - Installer: `/support/AXTRAXNG/AxtraxNG_27_7_1_20.zip` (~655 MB, dated 2025-07-01). - Documentation root: `/support/AXTRAXNG/documentation/`. - Available patches (under `/support/AXTRAXNG/Patches/`): - AC-825 Access Denied patch 20 - Access Denied patch (general) - AxtraxService 27_7_1_9 update - Keypad patch 27_7_1_19 - Patch_Files_27_5_7_16 - Patched_Client_19_photo - v27.7.1.18 patch **Panel compatibility:** AC-825IP and AC-215/225/425 (Series A and Series B). AC-215U must be manufactured after 01/01/2011. **Supported OS:** Windows 8.1, 10, 11; Windows Server 2012–2019; 32 or 64-bit. Windows XP and Windows 7 are NOT supported. **SQL Server:** 2012 through 2019. Does NOT support SQL Server 2005. **Minimum hardware:** Intel Core i5 dual-core 2.5 GHz, 8 GB RAM, 5 GB free storage. ### AxtraxPro (current / recommended) - Current version: **AxtraxPro 28.0.5.2** (release notes 2024-04-27; 28.0.5.0 dated 2024-12-26; datasheet 2024-12-23). - Installer: `/support/AXTRAXPRO/AxtraxPro_28_0_5_2.zip` (~680 MB, dated 2025-04-29). - Documentation root: `/support/AXTRAXPRO/Documentation/`. - Mobile app package available: `AxtraxPro_Mobile.zip`. **Panel compatibility:** AC-825IP (MCU **must be type VG**) and AC-215/225/425 Series A/B. **Supported OS:** Windows 10 or 11 64-bit; Windows Server 2016 or later. **SQL Server:** 2019 only. Does NOT support SQL Server 2012. **Minimum hardware:** Intel Core i5 dual-core 2.5 GHz, 8 GB RAM, 5 GB free storage. **Upgrade path:** Existing AxtraxNG server must already be running 27.7.1.18 or later. Verify AC-825IP MCU type VG in Panel Properties from NG 27.7.1.18+ before attempting the upgrade. --- ## 3. Licensing ### AxtraxNG Licensing - Licenses apply to video integration (AX-HIK / AX-DAU) and reader ports. - No license required for systems with fewer than **256 reader ports**. - Ordering a key requires the **HWID** of the server PC (obtain via `Rosslare_AxtraxNG_HWID_Utility.zip`; procedure in "AxtraxNG HWID.pdf"). - Activation bulletin: "How To Activate AxTraxNG License.pdf". ### AxtraxPro Licensing - Licenses apply to video integration, web clients, and reader ports. - No license required for systems with fewer than **6 reader ports**. - Separate `Rosslare_AxTraxPRO_HWID_Utility.zip` generates Pro HWID (procedure in "AxtraxPro HWID.pdf"). - Pricing-model PDFs on site: `AxTraxPro_Licenses.pdf` and `AxTraxPro_Licensing_Packages.pdf` (under `/support/axtraxpro/documentation/Pricing model/`). ### Video Integration License Order Models (AxtraxNG) From `/support/axtraxng/documentation/AxtraxNG Video Integration.txt`: **Hikvision NVR (AX-HIK-Lx)** — L1: 2 cameras/1 NVR · L2: 4 cameras/2 DVRs · L3: 9 cameras/4 DVRs · L4: 16 cameras/8 DVRs · L5: unlimited cameras/12 DVRs. **Dahua NVR (AX-DAH-Lx)** — L1: 2 cameras/1 DVR · L2: 4 cameras · L3: 9 cameras/4 DVRs · L4: 16 cameras/8 DVRs · L5: unlimited cameras/12 DVRs. **Supported SDK versions:** Hikvision 5.3.2.15; Dahua 3.4.8.27787. --- ## 4. Installation Notes (AxtraxNG 27.7.1.2X) Derived from `Installing_AxtraxNG_27_7_1_2X.pdf`: **Prerequisites** - Windows 7 SP1, 8.1, or 10 (32 or 64-bit) per the installer doc. (Landing-page copy states 8.1/10/11 — the installer PDF is older and also lists Win 7 SP1; treat 10/11 as current.) - .NET Framework 4.5+. - SQL Server 2012 or later (SQL Server Express 2019 bundled for new installs). - Static IP addresses on the AxtraxNG server and any AC-825 panels. - Broadcast (ARP) and UDP must be allowed between server and panels on the same subnet. **Antivirus / firewall** - Quoted verbatim: *"Microsoft Windows Defender is the ONLY antivirus software we have tested with and will support."* - McAfee and Symantec are "not supported and not recommended." - Windows Firewall is required; Rosslare "does not recommend disabling the firewall." **Install sequence** 1. Extract the installer ZIP. 2. Right-click `AxtraxNGSetup.EXE` and run **as Administrator** (mandatory). 3. Select "Server with Server Monitor and Client" (Client-only option exists for remote workstations). 4. Accept defaults through Client → SQL Server → Server → Server Monitor sub-installers. 5. Do NOT select AxTime, Vitrax, or Vitrax LPR (deprecated). 6. No reboot required. 7. Post-install: apply the firewall rule (below). **Upgrade rules** - 27.x over 27.x or later (SQL 2012+ present): allowed; database upgrades automatically. - Older than 27.x or coming from AxtraxAS525: **contact Technical Support first**; SQL 2005 instances cannot be reused. - 27.7.1.20 over 24.x with SQL2012+: skip the bundled SQL 2019 install. **Pre-upgrade backup (critical)** 1. In the AxtraxNG client: Tools → Database → "Backup Now". 2. In Task Manager, stop both `Server Monitor Process` and `AxtraxServerService` before running the installer — otherwise the service and monitor will not be upgraded. 3. After upgrade, open the client and push firmware to all panels (panels may flag "incompatible firmware" — click Yes to add, then update firmware). **Remote client** - Supported. Initial panel discovery must happen on the same physical LAN/subnet; remote management via VPN/WAN works thereafter. --- ## 5. Firewall Configuration From `/support/axtraxng/documentation/firewall_rule.txt`: After install, right-click `firewall.cmd` (in the extracted installer directory, e.g. `Downloads\AxtraxNG_27_7_x.x`) and **Run as administrator**. If that fails or a remote client / AC-825 can't initialize, create the inbound rule manually: 1. Control Panel → Windows Defender Firewall → Advanced Settings. 2. Inbound Rules → New Rule → Program. 3. Program path: `C:\Program Files (x86)\Rosslare\AxtraxNG Server\AxtraxService.exe` (64-bit OS) or `C:\Program Files\Rosslare\AxtraxNG Server\AxtraxService.exe` (32-bit OS). 4. Action: Allow the Connection. Keep all profiles selected. 5. Name: `axtraxng`. A separate `AxtraxPro_firewall.zip` exists under `/support/AXTRAXPRO/` for Pro. --- ## 6. Panel Hardware Reference (from panels-B.html) ### AC-215IP-BU 2 doors, 2 reader ports, 2 REX inputs, 2 AUX/door inputs, 2 AUX outputs; no on-board expansion. RS-485/TCP-IP. 30 000 users, 20 000 events on board. 128 timezones. Power 90–265 VAC. Reader supply 300 mA @ 12 VDC. Lock supply 2.0 A @ 13.8 VDC. 12 V / 7 Ah battery support (~3 hr standby). 14.4" × 10.4" × 3.8", 8.4 lbs. Operating temp 32–120 °F. ### AC-225(IP)-BU 2/4 doors, 2/4 reader ports, 2/4 REX inputs, 2/4 AUX/door inputs, 2/4 AUX outputs. Expansion: one MD-D02B. RS-485 (plus TCP/IP on IP model). Capacity, power, enclosure same as AC-215 family. ### AC-425(IP)-BU 4/8 doors, 4/8 reader ports, 4/8 REX inputs. Expansion: one MD-D04B. RS-485 (plus TCP/IP on IP model). Note: in 1-reader-per-door mode there are no door-monitor/AUX inputs or outputs available; in 2-reader-per-door mode REX inputs 2 and 4 can be used as door-monitor or AUX inputs. ### AC-825(IP) 4/8 doors (standard/expanded), 4/8 reader ports, 4/8 REX inputs, 6/10 AUX/door inputs, 2 AUX outputs. 60 000 users, **500 000 events** stored on board. 256 timezones. TCP/IP only. Power 100–240 VAC @ 1.6 A. Reader supply 500 mA, lock supply **2.5 A @ 13.8 VDC** (requires jumper from PSU board to relays). 12 V / 7 Ah battery. 13.6" × 15.9" × 4.0", 10.1 lbs. Operating temp 23–122 °F. Expansion for AC-825 is OSDP-attached and includes up to **12 expansion modules**: - **D-805** — adds 4 doors/readers. - **R-805** — adds 16 Form-C relay outputs. - **S-805** — adds 16 supervised inputs. - **P-805** — adds 8 Form-C outputs + 16 supervised inputs. Two OSDP readers can be added to the base board to bring the standard 4-reader capacity up to 6 without an expansion. **Legacy note:** Series A panels (AC-215F, AC-225, AC-425 Rev A) are discontinued. Use Rev B; contact Tech Support for interoperability, software update may be required. **RS-485 / Ethernet runs:** RS-485 up to 2 000 ft on 22-ga 120 Ω STP; Ethernet 330 ft on Cat5e/Cat6. AxtraxNG supports 32 networks × up to 32 panels = **1 023 panels max**. --- ## 7. Expansion Modules (Series B) Compatibility | Module | Compatibility | Function | |---|---|---| | MD-D02B | AC-225-BU | 2-door expansion | | MD-D04B | AC-425-BU | 4-door expansion | | MD-IO84B | AC-225-BU & AC-425-BU | 0 doors, 4 inputs, 4 relays | | D/R/S/P-805 | AC-825IP | See §6 | --- ## 8. AxtraxPro-Only Content on the Site Brochures: `AxTraxPro-brochure-upd-V010.pdf`, plus subfolders for AxtraxPro Development Tools, Milestone XProtect VMS integration, and Hikvision MinMoe biometric terminals. Technical notes and integration guides (under `/support/axtraxpro/documentation/Technical notes and guides/`): - `AxTraxPro+IDEMIA+Biometric+On+Card+Integration+Guide+V2024_04_01.pdf` - `AxTraxPro+KONE+Elevator+Control+Integration+Guide+V2024_02_06.pdf` - `Hikvision+MinMoe+Setup+Guide+V2023_09_28.pdf` - Desktop-client, Web-client, and Milestone / Hikvision MinMoe integration sub-folders. - `Technical Note Rolling Back from AxTraxPro to AxTraxNG V2022_03_20.pdf` - `Technical Note Rosslare OSDP Support Commands V2022_04_06.pdf` - `Upgrade Guide from AxTraxNG to AxTraxPro V2022_03_20.pdf` NDAA compliance PDF (`NDAA Compliance and AxTraxPro Integration.pdf`, dated 2025-08-29) is posted under both `/support/axtraxng/documentation/` and `/support/AXTRAXPRO/Documentation/`. --- ## 9. Training & Installation Guides (from training.html) PDF guides: - Hardware Installation Guide (`installation_hardware.pdf`) - AxtraxNG Installation Guide (`installing_axtraxNG_27_7_1_2X.pdf`) - Creating Networks & Finding Panels (`installing_create_networks.pdf`) - Creating ACLinks / Automations (`installing_ACLinks.pdf`) - Managing Users and Access Groups (`installing_manage_users.pdf`) - Managing Databases incl. Upgrades (`installing_backup-restore.pdf`) - Setup Antipassback and Parking (`installing_antipassback-parking.pdf`) - Installing Remote Client (`installing_remote_client_27.pdf`) - Moving AxtraxNG (`move_axtraxng.pdf`) - Firmware Update (`firmware.pdf`) - Replace AC-825IP SD Card (`AC-825_SD_Card_replacement.pdf`) - AC-825IP Wiring (`AC_825_wiring.pdf`) YouTube video guides: AxtraxNG Overview, Wiring an AC Panel, Adding Users/Credentials, Troubleshooting Connection Issues, Backups, Setting Up Automatic Door Unlock, Card Design, Car Park, Elevator Control, Standalone Controller Wiring, Standalone Controller Programming. --- ## 10. Manuals (manuals.html) Panels: AC-215, AC-215x-B, AC-215IP, AC-225x-B, AC-225x Rev A, AC-425x-B, AC-825IP, AC-115 (hardware & software), AC-Q4x, AC-F/G 43/44. Standalone: AYC-F/G/M 60, AYC-Q60, AYC-F/G 54/64, AYC-Q 54/64, AYC-x6355, AY-x6255, AY-H6355BT, AY-x12C, AY-x20. Readers: MD-N32, MD-N33, LK-M03, LK-M06L, LK-M12L, EX-06/16, EX-07/17, PS-C25T, MD-W11. Quick Guide: `Standalone_Quick_Guide.pdf`. Browse all: `/support/Manuals/`. --- ## 11. Tech Support Bulletins (`/support/tech_support/Tech_Support_Bulletins/`) Key bulletins available: - AxTraxNG client fails to capture user image using webcam - AxtraxNG HWID (2025-01-20) - axtraxng_date_2016 - AxtraxNG HIDPI display - AxtraxPro HWID (2025-01-20) - How To Activate AxTraxNG License - **How to set up Email Notification on AxTraxNG** - Tech Note AC-825IP bootloader v2.0 support for AxTraxNG 27.5.7.16 - Tech Note AxTraxNG Server Licensing Update (2016 original) - tech_note reset pw - Technical Bulletin — AxTraxNG to NVR Integration V1.3 (Jan 2019) - Technical Note AxTraxNG Log File Size - Technical Note AxTraxNG Setting Network Adapter Priority - Technical Note BLE Readers Password (2019-06-24) - Technical Note Firmware update procedure for X-805 expansion units (2020-12-10) - Technical Note FW Update with RS-485 (2022-03-23) - Technical Note MD-N32 v1.2.4 lost communication (2019-06-11) --- ## 12. Other Support Files (`/support/tech_support/`) - `AC-825 SD card replacement.pdf` - `AY-K35_V3_FW.zip` — AY-K35 V3 firmware - `AY-X12C wiring.PNG` - `CSN vs O2S.pdf` — card-number format comparison - `D-805.pdf` - `Dipswith Settings.pdf` - `Install_SQL_2005.pdf`, `Install_SQL_2012.pdf` - `No_FC_26bit_card_format.pdf` — no-facility-code 26-bit card format - `Programing a Stand Alone Reader.pdf` - `QueryExPlus.zip` — SQL query utility - `RSP_2014_Access_Control_Catalog_Credentials_Appendix.pdf` - `Single-Product-Warranty2-May2019.pdf` - `SQL/` folder and `SQL2019.zip` (~267 MB full SQL 2019 installer) - `Start Services.pdf` - `toggle main relay(standalone).txt` - `vcredist_x86.zip` - `Wiring Diagram.pdf` Top-level `/support/` also exposes product-specific folders: `AC-825IP`, `AS-B01`, `AS-IP01`, `AY-U920BT`, `CPR-27`, `CSN_Select`, `Digitool`, `MD-14U`, `MD-N32`, `Network_Config_Tool`, `Utilities`, plus `Product_Warranty.pdf`. --- ## 13. Sunset / Migration Strategy Summary AxtraxNG is Rosslare's legacy on-prem access-control platform, now in formal sunset. 27.7.1.20 is the last release, downloads stop 12/31/2026, and support ends 12/31/2027. Rosslare is pushing customers to AxtraxPro 28.x, which shares the same panel families (AC-825IP, AC-215/225/425) but requires Windows 10/11 64-bit, SQL Server 2019, and — for AC-825IP — an MCU revision type VG. Licensing thresholds dropped dramatically: Pro requires a license above 6 reader ports versus 256 on NG, so almost every production deployment needs a license post-migration. Both products use HWID-bound licensing tied to the server PC. Standard migration workflow: verify NG is at 27.7.1.18+, confirm each AC-825IP reports MCU type VG in Panel Properties, back up the NG database, stop NG services, run the AxtraxPro installer, migrate the DB, re-issue licenses against the new HWID, and update panel firmware. A rollback procedure exists ("Technical Note Rolling Back from AxTraxPro to AxTraxNG V2022_03_20"). --- ## 14. Contact Information (North American Tech Support) - Tech Support email: support.na@rosslaresecurity.com - Sales email: sales.na@rosslaresecurity.com - Phone: 866-632-1101 - TeamViewer QuickSupport download link is on the landing page. - Knowledge base: https://support.rosslaresecurity.com/ - YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCUdUvwv9mUkVvuxwaP280dQ - Tech Support Registration (required): Zoho form linked from the landing page. - Mailing / shipping (new address): - Synerion USA - 1600 Hart St, Suite 103 - Southlake, TX 76092

files.content: Login and Initial Setup Password issue resolved - caps lock was on from earlier license entry work Standard password noted: Capital R-O-S-S-9876 for most systems Mini PC password: Capital R, Zero, SSLMAR3 Configuring Hexadecimal Card Display Demonstrated how to enable hex view: Tools > Options > Cards Presentation > Hexadecimal When enabled, cards display in hex format under Users > Cards tab Current events continue to show in decimal, but new events display in hex Understanding 26-Bit Wiegand Format In 26-bit format, hex values can be concatenated directly to get the complete card number Example shown: F27F4F represents the entire card contents in order This format was used for a Florida client (A1) Working with Different Card Bit Lengths Challenge: Wiegand panels ignore cards of incorrect bit length entirely Advantage: Mickey added feature that displays bit length in log when wrong format is used (e.g., "access denied, invalid card, 37 bit") Solution: Enable secondary format on reader to test different bit lengths Can enable multiple formats simultaneously to identify unknown cards Card Format Flexibility Example 34-bit card can be presented different ways depending on data structure : 8 bits + 24 bits = facility code 0-255 with large card number 16 bits + 16 bits = alternative valid format Format selection depends on matching what's physically printed on the card Creating Custom Card Formats Custom formats can be created in the system settings Example demonstrated: 26-bit format with no facility code : Structure: P (parity bit) + 8 F's or zeros (where facility code normally is) + 16 bits data + P (parity bit) Notation: P = parity bit, F = facility code area, D = data Parity bits can be cloned from standard 26-bit format but often not necessary Trial and error approach works - experimenting with formats won't break anything Historical Context: Wiegand Technology Format originated in Northern Europe Original method: magnetized steel wire strips glued to cards 26-bit was chosen because it was the maximum that could fit reliably on credit card-sized cards Parity structure: first parity bit for first 13 bits, second for last 13 bits Parity was crucial for early magnetic stripe readers but less important with modern RFID Reference Resources Multiple old card format reference files available on the system RS2 (competitor) previously maintained excellent card format reference website RS2 was acquired and website is no longer accessible Site contained documentation for obscure formats and was regularly updated Alternative reference site found but not as comprehensive as RS2

files.snippet: Panel Discovery and Network Setup Panel discovery works by sending broadcast messages to devices with matching MAC addresses on the local LAN segment Discovery only works on the same VLAN and subnet - will not work across VPNs or subnets due to broadcast traffic limitations Two MAC address prefixes ...
files.content: Panel Discovery and Network Setup Panel discovery works by sending broadcast messages to devices with matching MAC addresses on the local LAN segment Discovery only works on the same VLAN and subnet - will not work across VPNs or subnets due to broadcast traffic limitations Two MAC address prefixes identify panel types: F8BC for older legacy panels and 0008DC for newer panels New panels default to 192.168.1.1 subnet out of the box Baud Rate Configuration Default configuration requires manual adjustment - panels ship with mismatched settings that prevent communication Network adapter defaults to 115 baud while DIP switches default to 9600 baud Always set both to 9600 baud - higher speeds increase risk of firmware update failures If firmware update fails at higher speeds, panel boots to bootloader which only supports 9600 baud, requiring reconfiguration anyway The analogy used: "Sure you can get there faster if you go 150 miles an hour, but if you get pulled over, you're fucked" Network Adapter Types and Legacy Panels Legacy panel network adapter is an ethernet-to-serial converter module under the cover Baud rate setting in software tells the network adapter what speed to use for serial communication to the controller board Gateway types include MDIP32 (built-in modules on older panels) and MDN32 (external adapters or correct setting for new panels) Critical: New panels with 0008 MAC addresses must stay set to MDN32 - changing this breaks communication AC825 panels show different gateway type identification Port Forwarding for Remote Management Default port is 1000, but can be customized per panel For remote management, use port forwarding with different ports per panel: port 1000 to panel 1, 2000 to panel 2, etc. This allows multiple panels to appear at the same public IP address but be accessible via different ports Most installations use daisy chaining instead to avoid running RS-485 wire across large buildings Panel Addressing Physical DIP switches on panel set the address Address setting is necessary for daisy-chained panels but doesn't hurt anything on separate IP connections Two reader per door configuration shows as "two little bumps" in the interface vs. one for single reader Firmware Updates and Troubleshooting Firmware is embedded within the Axtrax software, not downloaded from external sources Firmware updates wipe panel memory and reload everything, even when pushing the same firmware version Troubleshooting recommendation: For persistent weird issues (schedules not working, etc.), push a firmware update to completely reset the panel Updates run faster at 115 baud (about 3 minutes for firmware plus database download) Database download time varies: handful of users takes ~3.5 minutes, 1500 users takes significantly longer Cumulex-specific note: Firmware update capability recently released, but availability of firmware files within software needs verification Database Synchronization Software only pushes users to panels where those users have access configured Individual user additions push just that user to the panel Troubleshooting trick: If users aren't working after adding, disable and re-enable the panel to force a full database push This is equivalent to rebooting the panel Panel Series Differences B-series panels are the newer generation with blue PCB boards and black enclosures Original panels have green boards with tan enclosures Axtrax Pro does not differentiate between B-series and original panels in the interface AC215 was the original bare board panel design Event Filtering and Logging Event filtering is configured at the panel level under Options > Event Filter Default configuration minimizes event logging - outputs only report when opened from PC Card reads are logged separately in the Access Events view, not Panel Events All events count toward the 10,000 event database limit Recommendation: For busy systems, disable non-critical event reporting (like door monitor contacts) to reduce database load For troubleshooting schedules, enable door open/closed events to verify schedule execution Door Configuration Options Manual Door Operation: Controls whether door appears in manual operation list Door Monitor Enabled: Shows door monitor contact status instead of lock relay status in software Automatic Relock: Door relocks when monitor contact closes (door shuts) rather than waiting for timer - critical for strikes and motorized bolts First Person Delay: On scheduled unlock, door only unlocks after first valid credential use after schedule start time Door Open Time: Default 4 seconds Extended Open Time: Per-user option for longer access time Door Held Open Alarm: Default 30 seconds, configurable Forced Open Alarm: Default immediate, can add delay to debounce wind-triggered false alarms Door Alarms and Sounders Alarms only generate event log entries by default - no automatic physical alarms AC Links required to trigger panel sounder or external devices Panel sounder has different tones: short chime for door open, longer bell tone for alarms Sounder automatically clears after set duration External sirens/relays require additional AC Links to clear the alarm state AC Links and Door Automation Door schedules: Source type is "Output" not "Door" (which is only for door alarms) Automatic Operation event auto-fills all required settings Select desired schedule (called "time zone" in interface) to control unlock timing Must create separate AC Link for each door - no bulk setup option Multi-Site and Time Zone Configuration Network-level time zone setting allows one instance to manage panels in different time zones Schedules are set normally in operator's local time - software handles time zone conversion New Pro release includes multi-site capability with role-based operator permissions Operators can be restricted to manage only specific networks/sites

files.snippet: Key Fobs & Credentials EM (125 kHz) Fobs Two shapes available: rectangular (TB/TL) and round Currently only stocked in black; previously available in blue and gray These have the worst read range of all fobs but are popular due to smaller size Rectangular fobs are less durable and break easily, espe...
files.content: Key Fobs & Credentials EM (125 kHz) Fobs Two shapes available: rectangular (TB/TL) and round Currently only stocked in black; previously available in blue and gray These have the worst read range of all fobs but are popular due to smaller size Rectangular fobs are less durable and break easily, especially on key rings Performance issue: placing on key ring introduces metal into the antenna coil, significantly detuning the RF signal Mifare Fobs Sharp tooth (rounded triangle) shape is the standard Mifare fob Until recently, all Mifare fobs were this shape A larger rectangular Mifare fob exists but few have been distributed Multiple flavors of Mifare fobs available Third-Party Credentials Some distributors (notably one in Florida) import generic fobs from China with full 24-bit numbers printed instead of split facility code/ID format Farpoint credentials are currently resold; hoping to replace with Rossler solutions Bluetooth Credentials & Licensing Farpoint's Bluetooth implementation is easier from user standpoint Farpoint license tied to phone MAC address - must purchase new license if phone is lost or upgraded Rossler's Bluetooth credentials are provided at no charge to end users This creates dealer resistance as they cannot profit from credential sales Card Formats & Facility Codes 26-bit Format Standards Standard format: 8-bit facility code + 16-bit ID Some international cards print entire 24-bit value instead of split format HID cards contain facility codes but don't print them on cards (security measure) Handling Missing Facility Codes For takeovers without facility code information, can validate only 16-bit ID portion Mercury/Cumulex: Use "Wiegand Zero" setting to ignore facility codes Axtrax: Create custom format called "26-bit no facility code" Essential for sites with multiple facility codes from different card orders Troubleshooting Card Numbers Use hexadecimal display option in Axtrax event log to diagnose card format issues Method: Read card in hex, convert to appropriate bit format, match to printed number Axtrax Software & Licensing Axtrax NG Free up to 256 reader ports (any reader port counts whether in use or not) Example: AT-825 has 6 ports (2 OSDP + 4 Wiegand) all count toward limit Level 1 and Level 2 licenses exist for larger deployments, mostly school districts Originally used USB dongle, now uses hardware ID Critical: NG hardware ID is different from Pro hardware ID - must use correct tool for each Only China office can create NG licenses; cannot be generated independently Axtrax Pro Licensing done via server in Israel Same reader port counting methodology as NG Video Integration Hikvision/Dahua Integration Requires NVR and camera-based licensing Available license levels (not fully documented in price list): L1: 2 cameras, 1 NVR L5: Unlimited cameras, up to 5 NVRs Part numbers: AX-HIC for Hikvision, AX-DAU for Dahua Functionality unreliable over time due to firmware updates Enables live view or snapshot pop-ups on card swipes or alarms Can trigger camera views on events like doors held open too long Few Dahua integrations sold; most experience is with Hikvision Biometric Readers Discontinued: B8000 Series & 9350 B8000 series: Discontinued, poor quality, 2-year warranty expired on existing units AY-9350 (facial recognition): EOL'd two years ago Major limitation: Only stored one reference image per user Failed in multi-reader installations with different lighting conditions Current: 9000 Series All models include Bluetooth support using Rossler app AY-9120: Fingerprint + EM cards + Bluetooth AY-9150: Fingerprint + Mifare (CSN mode) + Bluetooth AY-9250: Fingerprint + EM or Mifare + Bluetooth + built-in snapshot camera Camera stores images locally when card/fingerprint used Higher price point limits adoption Images cannot trigger live pop-ups (stored only) General Notes Biometric readers are AY (reader only), not standalone controllers Not outdoor rated Moderate lifespan even in indoor installations Discontinued Products AX Time Time and attendance module Still appears in installer but no longer available Part number: AX-TM Could not handle time frames spanning midnight Predated Cenarion acquisition If customers inquire: Not available, cannot issue licenses, refer complex issues to Mickey Vitrax (V-Tracks) Third-party PC-based NVR with pop-up integration Required separate license purchase Performance issues: Extremely slow even on high-spec hardware (i7, SSD, 16GB RAM) Pop-up delays made system unusable for real-time monitoring No longer sold Other Readers AY-C and AY-D series: Indoor-only, inexpensive readers - not recommended E-series (AY-E12, AY-E20): Ultra-slim readers with aesthetic bezels Do not work well on metal door frames due to proximity to metal E-series keypads have piezo pressure sensitivity issues causing multi-key triggering Not recommended AY-E20: Could output Wiegand, Serial, or Clock-and-Data - discontinued years ago O2S Secure Readers Rossler's answer to HID iClass Uses Mifare in secure mode with pre-programmed credentials required Part numbers should include "O2S" in description Security Features 6270/6370 Series: 48-bit security key (standard Mifare) 128-bit encryption on card data Proprietary keys never released publicly 6280/6380 Series: Adds DESFire security chip layer Additional keys required before accessing encrypted data While theoretically clonable, extremely difficult - cannot be cloned at typical services like Home Depot Low market adoption but provides highest security option Product Naming Conventions AY: Reader only (peripheral to access control panel) AYC: Convertible reader/controller (requires special power supply) Naming inconsistencies exist throughout product line

files.snippet: Licensing Model Most Axtrax licensing is one-time fee, with the exception of the new Axtrax Pro Secure feature Axtrax Pro Secure uses a subscription model priced per operator (not per user) at approximately $5-$10/month, billed annually Dealers and distributors will receive a portion of the annual s...
files.content: Licensing Model Most Axtrax licensing is one-time fee, with the exception of the new Axtrax Pro Secure feature Axtrax Pro Secure uses a subscription model priced per operator (not per user) at approximately $5-$10/month, billed annually Dealers and distributors will receive a portion of the annual subscription fee as recurring revenue Axtrax Pro Secure Feature Includes a secured connection option that uses a VPN connection built into the Axtrax Pro software itself The VPN connects to a secure proxy server/portal in the cloud, which then connects to the local system via VPN Users log into the cloud portal (via SSL or similar), which maintains the VPN connection to the local system The subscription management is handled by the cloud proxy, not the local server The remote secure operators option must be enabled in the Axtrax Pro license, but the subscription is purchased separately License Management Licenses are looked up by hardware ID License files are generated and sent to customers Additional features (access ports, web clients) can be added to existing licenses Access Point Licensing Optimization Customers should minimize the total number of license items to reduce costs Example: For 44 licenses, buying one 32-license and one 16-license package may be cheaper than buying 32 + 8 + 4, even with unused licenses Sales teams may not always optimize these configurations Integration License Options All of the following are one-time enable/disable licenses (not quantity-based): HIC Vision Biometrics: Enables support for Hick Vision biometric terminals Idemia Biometrics: Enables support for Idemia biometric terminals Milestone X Protect: Interfaces with Milestone video surveillance system, enables pop-ups and user management from Milestone Bass IP Intercom: Cloud-based intercom system for apartment buildings, allows phone-based intercom calls; developed with funding from SAS Coney Elevators/Destination Control: Connects to Coney smart controllers over network for elevator control, allows user synchronization between systems STID Biometrics: Another biometric reader option LDAP: Enables connection to LDAP servers to download cardholder information (not operators); requires backend configuration to fully populate credential data Attendance Report: Time and attendance feature (details unclear)

files.snippet: Used Equipment Support Policy Company does not support used equipment purchased from unauthorized sources (eBay, resellers) Policy must be communicated clearly and firmly to customers, especially repeat offenders Discontinued products like mini PCs are explicitly not supported regardless of source W...
files.content: Used Equipment Support Policy Company does not support used equipment purchased from unauthorized sources (eBay, resellers) Policy must be communicated clearly and firmly to customers, especially repeat offenders Discontinued products like mini PCs are explicitly not supported regardless of source Warranty status and purchase through authorized distribution are key factors Specific case: Phil at High Tech (installer in New York) repeatedly purchases used equipment and requests support - has been denied Firmware Management 825 Panel Firmware: 825 panels ship with NG firmware by default Cumulex requires pro firmware on 825 panels Pro firmware can now be pushed through Cumulex as of last Thursday Previous workflow required pre-flashing panels before shipment Keep pro-firmware panels in stock for Cumulex demos and deployments Firmware Rollback Process: Rolling back from pro to NG firmware on 825s is complex and error-prone Requires specific dip switch patterns and timing Must power up, wait 60-90 seconds, flip dip switch 1 off while powered to erase firmware Process has high failure rate - boards can brick even when following instructions correctly Legacy panels can roll back firmware without issues Encryption and security features in pro firmware prevent easy rollback Future Considerations: 825s may start shipping with pro firmware after end of year (unannounced change expected) This will create challenges for customers wanting to use NG Sample and Demo Equipment Significant equipment cost per demo kit: approximately $500 including 825 ($250-300), readers ($25-30 each), and accessories Concern about ROI on demo equipment - many dealers receive demos but don't activate or use them Approximately half of Cumulex demo boxes sent to dealers are offline or never came online SQL Database Management Common Issues: Event history grows indefinitely by default in NG Databases can grow to 10+ GB with event data dating back to mid-2000s SQL Server Express has hard limit of 10,000 records per table When event table exceeds limits, various failures occur Event Storage Limits: Current limit in software: maximum 100 days No clear reason for 100-day limit - should ideally support at least one year Large systems (e.g., Loretto School District with 1000+ doors) limit to 60-90 days to prevent database bloat Database Troubleshooting Tools: SQL Server Studio (full-featured, heavyweight) SQL Query Plus (lightweight, requires .NET Framework) Scripts available in SQL tools directory for common fixes Script to check table sizes and identify full tables Script to delete old events and messages Password reset script for operator accounts Database Restoration: Clear database (empty template) used for new installations Wiping database restores the clear database, removing all data Directory structure from original backup can cause restoration failures Multiple SQL versions in use (2012, 2019, 2022) - databases cannot be restored to older versions Password Encryption: Passwords encrypted in database starting with version 27.7.1.18 or .19 Upgrade process encrypts passwords but may not fix username capitalization Administrator username must have capital 'A' to match encrypted format Only way to reset passwords is through SQL script Panel Deletion Behavior: Deleting panel from database removes all references across all tables Clears from access groups, AC links, and all associations Access group structure and time zones remain, but linkages to panel are removed Must rebuild all associations when re-adding panel Panel Management AC225IP Replacement: Can only replace A-series panel with PCBA board, not complete unit NG cannot swap B-series for A-series panels - requires database rebuild Pro allows mixing A and B daughterboards - no differentiation in database Pro panels can be swapped (B for A) with just IP reconfiguration NDA Process for API/SDK Access Requirements: All API/SDK access requires signed NDA Customer must complete comprehensive NDA request form Form requires company registration number, tax ID, and detailed information Process managed through Boris (sales), previously through legal department Workflow: Customer requests API/SDK access Technical support sends NDA request form to customer Customer completes and returns form Form forwarded to Boris (or designated contact) Israel generates NDA with customer information Customer electronically signs Mickey provides API access directly to customer API Support: Mickey provides all API support - not handled by technical support Documentation available at RossLairSecurity.stoplight.io API allows most functionality available in client application Increased volume of NDA requests in recent year(s) - went from 1-2/year to 4-5 simultaneously Known Integrations: NEXLAR (Houston) built web front-end using NG SDK Elevator Control Implementation Standard Configuration: Each relay assigned to one elevator button One panel required per elevator cab Relay board required per cab AC215 panels support up to 4 floors (4 outputs) Setup Process: Create output groups for each floor/combination Create master output group with all floors for building supervisors Configure AC link with "output group selected by user" option Assign output groups to individual users Set timer duration for output activation Limitations: All outputs in user's assigned group fire simultaneously - no time-based scheduling per floor Cannot assign output groups to specific readers All R805 expansion boards on same 825 panel fire together (cannot isolate by cab) Alternative Integration: Kone integration available (server-to-server communication) Completely different implementation from standard relay-based control System Compatibility Notes Cumulex Integration: Larry has been out of loop on Cumulex development and testing No knowledge of Jessica's current integration status or capabilities 825-only support is key differentiator Elevator control may have differences in implementation and capabilities

files.snippet: Summary Axtrax Pro integrates with KONE Destination Control Server using API 500 Connectivity Suite over HTTPS. Axtrax Pro retrieves fixed configuration data from the KONE server, then sends user credentials and elevator access rights back to KONE. Elevator control is done via KONE smart readers and...
files.content: Summary Axtrax Pro integrates with KONE Destination Control Server using API 500 Connectivity Suite over HTTPS. Axtrax Pro retrieves fixed configuration data from the KONE server, then sends user credentials and elevator access rights back to KONE. Elevator control is done via KONE smart readers and the KONE controller. Rosslare panels still handle doors and gates. System requirements Valid annual license with an expiry date (required by KONE). Axtrax Pro v28.0.2.8 or higher. Network connectivity to the KONE server (IP or URL) with correct credentials. Core KONE concepts (as seen in Axtrax Pro) Access Domains: Elevator groupings (by lobby, zone, area) that determine which elevators a user can use. Access Rights Groups: Floor sets within an access domain that a user is allowed to access. Time Zones: Schedules defining when floors are accessible. User Categories: KONE-defined user classifications (similar to departments). Home Floors: Optional single-floor assignment for users who only need one floor. Call Types (behavior when card is used): Normal Handicap (longer door open) Priority Empty car Space allocation Setup: connect Axtrax Pro to KONE In Axtrax Pro, open the new Elevator Control section. Click Edit to open the Elevator Configuration window. Enter server details and credentials, then Connect to retrieve configuration from the KONE server. Data retrieved (read-only in Axtrax): Access Domains, Access Rights, Time Zones, User Categories, Home Floors, Call Types. User setup in Axtrax Pro (Elevators tab) Elevator access rights can be configured for existing users, new users, or visitors. Credential format limitation noted in training: 56-bit Wiegand cards only. Fields and settings Elevator User ID: Issued by the KONE server after saving (initially blank). Elevator Settings: Which KONE server connection to use (can support multiple buildings). User Category: Select the KONE-defined category. Home Floor: Optional. Call Type: Choose desired elevator behavior. Access rights assignment flow Open a user → Elevators tab. Set the user’s credentials (56-bit Wiegand). Choose elevator settings, user category, home floor (optional), and call type. Add Access Domains and corresponding Access Rights Groups. Set validity date range for elevator access rights. Click OK to save and push to KONE. Validation / verification After saving, confirm that an Elevator User ID appears for the user. Use the Validate button (available after save completes) to verify settings on the KONE server. Review the events log at the bottom of Axtrax Pro for success or failure messages. Architecture notes (what talks to what) KONE smart readers with touchscreens are required for elevator control. They communicate with the KONE elevator controller. Rosslare AC-825 panels handle door and gate access and do not connect to elevator control. Axtrax Pro remains the management point for assigning both door access and elevator floor permissions. Q&A: known challenges and roadmap Local KONE teams may not know which API version (500, 1000, 5000) is installed at a site. To confirm API details, contact the site’s KONE server administrator. Current integration uses API 500. API 1000 integration is expected in the coming months. Future API 5000 is expected to shift more management from the KONE server to the Axtrax Pro server.

files.snippet: MIFARE Card Security Levels CSN (Card Serial Number) Mode All MIFARE readers initially read only the card serial number (CSN or UID) Available as 4-byte or 7-byte UIDs No encryption or security - can be read with any NFC-enabled phone 7-byte UIDs always start with "04" Many readers still operate in ...
files.content: MIFARE Card Security Levels CSN (Card Serial Number) Mode All MIFARE readers initially read only the card serial number (CSN or UID) Available as 4-byte or 7-byte UIDs No encryption or security - can be read with any NFC-enabled phone 7-byte UIDs always start with "04" Many readers still operate in CSN-only mode out of the box Security level comparable to a Kwikset door key - easily cloned Sector Mode Uses 48-bit security key (called "Key A" in MIFARE terminology) Cards can be formatted into sectors (max 16 sectors on 1K cards) Each sector can have a unique key Data is not encrypted by default unless specifically encrypted when written Designed to allow multiple applications on one card (medical info, banking credentials, etc.) 48-bit keys can be cracked in a few hours with proper equipment Used in some biometric readers to store biometric data on the card O2S Encrypted Cards Adds 128-bit encryption on top of 48-bit key Data is encrypted and format is locked Significantly more secure - cracking takes much longer Could not be copied at common card-cloning kiosks Company maintains control of encryption keys and sells pre-programmed cards Reader Models and Capabilities K35 Reader Reads CSN by default (both 4-byte and 7-byte UIDs) Has setting to "Ignore first byte on 7 byte CSN" to match other readers Sector mode available but not enabled by default Configured through settings interface Must specify sector, block, and Key A to read secured data K-30 Reader Present in the facility Appears to be an earlier model 6255/6300 Series (B Series) Originally CSN-only readers (4-byte only initially) B series models support sector mode via configuration card tool Configuration tool available for download Still sold as CSN-only readers for customers wanting simple, inexpensive solutions Card Structure and Programming MIFARE Card Organization Cards organized into sectors and blocks Example: 16 blocks per sector Programming requires specifying sector, block address, and Key A Data written in hexadecimal format Available in 1K, 4K, 8K, and 16K capacities Multi-Application Use Case Theoretically, one card can hold credentials for multiple locations Each location uses different sector or address within sector Requires coordination of formats across locations Rarely implemented in practice Security and Cloning Concerns Vulnerability of Basic Cards CSN-only MIFARE cards as vulnerable as 125 kHz HID/EM cards Easily cloned with Android phones and apps like Tag Writer Kiosks in grocery stores can clone 125 kHz cards, driving interest in MIFARE Many people incorrectly assume MIFARE is inherently more secure HID Comparison HID iClass uses similar sector-based approach with proprietary formats and controlled keys HID now using 256-bit encryption on higher-end cards Original HID patents expired, making HID prox cards free-for-all HID more dominant in US market; MIFARE more popular internationally HID Corporate 1000 formats still proprietary with custom formatting Configuration Tools and Resources Mobile Apps for MIFARE NXP Tag Info app (Android only) - displays card information and format NXP Tag Writer app - can write data to cards iPhone NFC radio not accessible for these purposes Configuration Card Tool Used to configure 6255/6300 series readers for sector mode Downloadable from company website or Rossler Security Home Automation Integration NFC tags can be used with Home Assistant and similar systems Same MIFARE credentials work with company's NFC readers NFC stickers can serve as credentials (1K tags have enough memory for business card data) All NFC and MIFARE operate at 13.56 MHz frequency Historical Context Legacy Issues Old sector readers (ASB 01 programmer) had firmware issue reading data backwards Could not be fixed without ordering 1,000+ readers for custom firmware Very few customers used sector readers until recent years Recent Adoption MIFARE reader usage has increased significantly in last few years Still relatively uncommon in some market segments

files.snippet: Product Line Evolution The 6255 series CSN Select readers replaced an older product line that had model numbers like K-H-J-25 The older models looked identical to current AYK-12 units in form factor but were fully potted with no removable backs When the company started, there were 12-14 different mo...
files.content: Product Line Evolution The 6255 series CSN Select readers replaced an older product line that had model numbers like K-H-J-25 The older models looked identical to current AYK-12 units in form factor but were fully potted with no removable backs When the company started, there were 12-14 different models including the 20 and 25 series, with variations K, H, J, L, and Q (anti-vandal metal housing) Most variations were plastic molded units that looked identical from the outside - you had to check the back label or wiring to differentiate Most older models have been discontinued except the AYK-12 Protocol and Feature Differences The model 14 used clock and data protocol, which was more common historically but is rarely used now The model 20 was multi-functional for reading EM cards with a control lead that could switch between three output modes: Wiegand (floating), RS-232 (grounded), or clock and data (tied high) Despite the 20's functionality, the simpler and cheaper model 12 sold better All the multi-protocol functionality from the 20 was eventually rolled into the 12C, which has been available for years The company kept the "12" model number because it was well-known and sold well, rather than rebranding as a new 20 series Form Factors L form factor is a tall mullion reader (L for "long") The J form factor was in-between K and L lengths but has been discontinued The L version is still produced for some models like the 6255 Some old part numbers may still appear in documentation though the products haven't been manufactured in years L23G Remote System The L23G is a receiver for remote fobs, similar in concept to garage door openers Each fob generates a unique 26-bit code like a credential The receiver has two Wiegand outputs (A and B) allowing control of two different doors based on which button is pressed Two-button remotes output different codes per button by default (e.g., 1201 and 1202) Four-button remotes output unique codes per button but all on channel A only A clever installation used button IDs with access groups in software to control multiple doors, with each button mapped to a different door Florida Condo Installation Case Study First deployment of AC-825 panels at a high-end condo in Florida used for valet parking garage access The remote system was used by valets to open various parking garages across approximately eight panels The multi-button setup created chaotic event logs with access denied entries on all panels except the authorized one Installation was sold through EPCOT initially, and Mariano came from South America to help install (Brad sponsored the visa) The 825 panels had issues due to improper RS-485 wiring spread across Miami RS-485 Wiring Challenges Industry specifications called for RS-485 wiring with 120 ohm, 18 gauge twisted pair cable, but this cable does not exist The closest available option is Belden's RS-485 cable: 22 gauge, twisted pair, controlled impedance, 120 ohm, with drain/ground wire This specialized cable is expensive, so most installations don't use it There was a long-running debate about cable specifications, with Mickey insisting on 18 gauge 18 gauge cable is power wire with near-zero impedance, unsuitable for RS-485 data transmission The company eventually approved Cat5 or Cat6 cable with proper wiring: data on one pair, ground on another twisted pair, with unused conductors grounded to reduce noise The Florida installation had used 18 gauge twisted wire that couldn't carry RS-485 signals beyond 100 feet (total run was ~1000 feet) The system was flaky until complete rewiring was required The installer was initially upset but appreciated having a clear explanation of the problem Firmware Update and Troubleshooting Practices Recommending firmware updates is often intentional, not busy work - it clears corrupted flash memory in panels Legacy panels especially can develop memory corruption from electrical issues, lightning, or glitches over time Firmware updates clear all panel memory, which often resolves persistent issues If one component is corrupted, there are likely more problems - firmware update catches all issues at once Mercury boards have a similar practice: rebooting the panel pushes the entire config block rather than just changes, which can resolve corrupted components like schedules In the past, firmware updates were risky to perform, but current practices are safer Office Setup Note A door alert system was set up for Terry so she would know when someone entered through the front door, particularly when working alone in the mornings

files.snippet: Firmware and Software Compatibility Current software enforces firmware version matching, unlike previous versions which allowed running antiquated firmware with newer software Mismatched versions can cause problems that are difficult to troubleshoot; rolling back software can be problematic as it of...
files.content: Firmware and Software Compatibility Current software enforces firmware version matching, unlike previous versions which allowed running antiquated firmware with newer software Mismatched versions can cause problems that are difficult to troubleshoot; rolling back software can be problematic as it often affects SQL server/database Firmware files are located in the server's program files under a firmware folder - this represents the minimum firmware version required for the software to work Firmware doesn't change between every software release; older panels like the 215 haven't had firmware updates in 5-6 years Newer firmware with older software generally works fine (unlike the reverse), particularly with 825 panels where firmware changes frequently Installation and Update Process The setup program (e.g., G20.20) can be extracted and run manually; extraction is the first step the installer performs anyway All components are embedded in the install package, including SQL server and firmware files When manually installing SQL first, the installer will still prompt for SQL installation - select "skip" in this case Newer firmware can be copied into the firmware directory to update panels to the latest version Port Forwarding Troubleshooting Use U-Get Signal (or similar tools) to check if port 1818 is open at remote sites Disable the network in software temporarily before testing to avoid false readings from active connection attempts Can test remote IP addresses from any location using the tool Documentation Resources UL listed product documentation is available in a cached location on the system Documentation includes product warranty documents and UL certificates dating back to 2015 B series panels (including 825) are covered in consolidated UL documents, though boards themselves aren't marked with "B" - only part numbers and boxes are Many readers and components historically were not UL listed

files.snippet: Access Groups & User Management Key Difference Between Pro and NG XTracks NG limitation: Each user can only be a member of one access group If you have 40 offices with unique access requirements, you need 40 separate access groups Different schedules require different access groups Example: A compan...
files.content: Access Groups & User Management Key Difference Between Pro and NG XTracks NG limitation: Each user can only be a member of one access group If you have 40 offices with unique access requirements, you need 40 separate access groups Different schedules require different access groups Example: A company with 95 employees ended up with 93 access groups due to varying schedules and access needs XTracks Pro Advantages Users can be assigned to multiple access groups simultaneously Build access rights from modular groups (common doors + specific areas) Can assign individual readers to users with custom schedules Example workflow: Assign common exterior doors group, then add specific lab access (Lab 1, Lab 2, etc.) as needed Much cleaner management for large organizations Time Zones & Schedules Each access group has a default time zone for all readers Individual readers within a group can have different time zones via dropdown selection Migration Considerations Importing from NG brings over all legacy access groups and structures Recommended approach: Build new modular access groups and gradually migrate users Multi-site implementations: Create site-specific groups first, then build role-based groups (e.g., "Fifth Street Manager," "Fifth Street Employee") Naming conventions become critical for long access group lists Security Features Lockdown Functionality Requires separate lock and unlock cards Different users can have lock-only vs. unlock privileges Immune users can be designated to maintain access during lockdowns Must designate an emergency unlock input on 825 panels (required in many European countries) Physical unlock button required by European regulations; physical lockdown button prohibited Lockdown can be triggered from software with proper operator permissions Common implementations: City/county offices and police stations Door Interlocks (Mantrap) Requires door contacts When one door is open, the other cannot be unlocked Common for jewelry stores and marijuana dispensaries 825 panels support multiple interlock groups with three or more doors Card Plus Card Requires two cards swiped sequentially on same reader for high-security areas Card plus card groups allow any two people from designated group to unlock door Access Control Features Access Areas & Anti-Passback Used primarily for anti-passback: must swipe out before swiping in again Supports muster reports to track who's in the building Car Parking Controls who can park in specific areas and when Tracks vehicle counts in parking areas Can trigger outputs when lot is full (e.g., "Lot Full" sign or gate disable) Visitor Management Visitors treated like a department with customizable access Can specify manager/host and visiting department Auto-disable options: When visitor exits area or building Card handling: Set to inactive, unauthorize user, or unassign card to pool User Administration User Filter Tool Quick search by name, department, user number, or card number Search by card ID (facility code not needed) to identify card owners View all users in specific access group Identification field (125 characters) is searchable - useful for apartment numbers or grouping Card Automation Automatically delete or inactivate unused cards after specified period Options: Notification only, queue for approval, or automatic action Generate reports of unused cards for cleanup ID Card Printing Built-in card design tool with drag-and-drop interface Supports portrait or landscape orientation Can include: User number, name, department, title, photo, logo Prints to default Windows printer Set card printer as default before opening client Can save designs and batch print Operators & Permissions Current Pro Version All operators must be mapped to a user Web client access requires license (one included, additional licenses available) Can now reset passwords with admin rights (unlike NG which requires deleting/recreating operator) Password requirements: Uppercase, lowercase, number, minimum 6 characters Permission Levels Event view: Modify time frames (read-only vs. none) Network access controls Lockdown permissions allow operator to apply/release lockdowns Status Maps & Monitoring Visual Door Monitoring Add doors to map in design mode Show lock relay status or door monitor (open/closed) Customize fonts and icon sizes Can import background drawings (limited file types, not PNG) Scaling challenges - best to build on display machine for large monitors Live status with right-click door control (unlock, lock, open permanently) During lockdown: Can unlock specific door temporarily to allow emergency access Reporting Immediate Reports Who's been in today Last known position Roll call/muster Panel Reports View any event type (inputs, outputs, access events) Sort by user or reader Time range options: "Last day" = yesterday (not last 24 hours); use Custom for 24-hour periods Event types: Access granted, access denied, access logged, access recorded Custom fields can be added Does not show which specific credential was used, only all credentials assigned to user System/Audit Reports Track all operator actions (card modifications, configuration changes, etc.) Customizable column arrangement and grouping Export formats: PDF, CSV (preferred for Excel manipulation) Warning: Excel export creates formatted multi-page report with merged cells - unusable for data analysis Best Practices For multi-site systems: Establish clear naming conventions early Plan access group structure before building For large migrations: Build new modular groups and migrate gradually Use CSV export (not Excel) for data analysis

files.content: Panel Not Connecting to Network Last Updated: May 1, 2026 Audience: All Users Products: AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, AC-Series Panels Overview If your access control panel won't appear in the software's configuration, or you can't locate it on your network, this guide will help you diagnose and resolve the connection issue. --- Symptoms You're experiencing one or more of the following: - Panel doesn't appear when searching in the software configuration - Panel worked before but disappeared from the network - Panel shows in the software list but won't respond to commands - Network connectivity indicator shows as offline or disconnected - Software simply won't locate the panel on the network --- Common Causes Panel Needs a Restart The panel has lost network synchronization and a simple power cycle restores connectivity. This is the most common fix. Fresh Installation or Replacement Panel New or replacement panels sometimes need a restart or firmware update to establish network communication. Network Changed by IT The network topology or IP addressing scheme was changed (common after IT infrastructure updates), and the panel hasn't reconnected yet. Panel Moved Physically The panel was moved to a different location or subnet but the network configuration wasn't updated. Firmware Not Installed New panels sometimes don't have firmware pre-installed and cannot communicate on the network until updated. --- Solutions Option 1: Power Cycle the Panel (Most Common Fix) A simple restart resolves most connectivity issues. Steps: 1. Locate the power supply connected to your panel 2. Unplug the power cable from the wall outlet or UPS 3. Wait 30 seconds (this ensures a full power drain) 4. Plug the power back in and wait for startup (typically 10-30 seconds) 5. Check the LED status light on the front of the panel (should be solid or slowly blinking green) 6. Try to locate the panel again in your software What to expect: The panel will display a startup sequence. Once fully booted, the network connectivity should restore automatically. --- Option 2: Test Panel Communication & Update Firmware if Needed If a power cycle didn't work, test whether the panel can communicate with your software. Steps: 1. In your access control software, go to Device Configuration or Panel Setup 2. Right-click on the panel and select Test or Verify Connection 3. If the test passes, the firmware is fine—move to Option 3 4. If the test fails, the panel cannot communicate and likely needs a firmware update: - Connect the panel directly to your computer using an Ethernet cable (bypassing your network if possible) - Right-click the panel and select Firmware Update - Click OK and allow 3-5 minutes for the update to complete - Once complete, the panel should restart and reconnect to your network What to expect: A successful test means the panel can communicate. If firmware update was needed, the panel may go offline briefly during the update, then automatically reconnect. --- Option 3: Verify Physical Connections & Basic Network Setup Check the hardware and basic network configuration. Steps: 1. Verify the Ethernet cable is connected: - Confirm the cable is fully inserted into both the panel's network port and your computer/switch - Look for a solid or blinking green light on the Ethernet port - Try a different Ethernet cable if one is available 2. Check if your computer can see the panel's IP: - In your software's Device Configuration, look for the panel's IP address - Try to ping that address from Command Prompt: type `ping [panel-ip-address]` - If ping works, the panel is reachable on your network 3. For AC-825 panels, check Windows Firewall settings: - Go to Control Panel > Windows Defender Firewall > Advanced Settings - Confirm that `AxtraxService.exe` has an inbound rule allowing connections - If no rule exists, create one: allow all connections for `AxtraxService.exe` 4. If nothing above worked, the panel may be on a different network than your computer: - This happens if the panel was moved to a different location or your IT department changed the network - Contact your IT department to verify both the panel and computer are on the same network segment - They may need to assign the panel a static IP address --- Option 4: Contact Your IT Department If the above steps didn't work, this is likely a network issue that requires IT support. Information to provide to IT: - Panel IP address (from your software's Device Configuration) - Panel model number (e.g., AC-215, AC-425, AC-825IP) - What changed recently (IT infrastructure updates, network changes, panel physically moved, etc.) - Current status: "My panel won't connect to the software" Common issues IT can resolve: - Panel is on a different network subnet than your computer - Network firewall rules are blocking communication - Panel needs a static IP address assignment - Network topology has changed since the panel was installed --- Network Specifications (Reference) For planning and troubleshooting purposes, here are the network specifications for Rosslare panels: System Scale: AxTraxNG supports up to 1,024 panels across multiple networks (32 networks × 32 panels each). --- Prevention - Update firmware regularly: New firmware includes network stability improvements and security patches - Use quality Ethernet cables: Ensure cables are CAT5e or higher and fully inserted on both ends - Use static IP addresses: Configure panels with static IPs to prevent conflicts and simplify troubleshooting - Maintain network documentation: Keep a record of each panel's IP address, location, and hardware type - Use a backup power supply: Connect panels to an uninterruptible power supply (UPS) to avoid sudden power loss during updates - Enable Windows Firewall: AxTraxNG/Pro requires Windows Firewall to be enabled and properly configured - Test connectivity regularly: Periodically verify all panels are reachable and responding - Restart routinely: If issues recur periodically, schedule monthly restarts during maintenance windows --- Still Having Issues? If none of these steps resolve your issue: - Note the panel model number and IP address (if visible) - Check the firmware version shown in the software - Document any error messages displayed - Note when the issue started and what changed (power outage, network changes, recent updates) - Contact support with this information at [support contact info] --- Related Articles - [How to Update Panel Firmware](KB_02_Firmware_Updates.md) - [Troubleshooting: Panel Shows Offline in Software](advanced-troubleshooting.md) - [Network Setup Guide for Access Control Panels](network-setup.md) - [IP Address Configuration for AC-225 Panels](ac225-ip-setup.md) --- Revision History

files.content: # Panel Not Connecting to Network **Last Updated:** May 1, 2026 **Audience:** All Users **Products:** AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, AC-Series Panels ## Overview If your access control panel won't appear in the software's configuration, or you can't locate it on your network, this guide will help you diagnose and resolve the connection issue. --- ## Symptoms You're experiencing one or more of the following: - Panel doesn't appear when searching in the software configuration - Panel worked before but disappeared from the network - Panel shows in the software list but won't respond to commands - Network connectivity indicator shows as offline or disconnected - Software simply won't locate the panel on the network --- ## Common Causes **Panel Needs a Restart** The panel has lost network synchronization and a simple power cycle restores connectivity. This is the most common fix. **Fresh Installation or Replacement Panel** New or replacement panels sometimes need a restart or firmware update to establish network communication. **Network Changed by IT** The network topology or IP addressing scheme was changed (common after IT infrastructure updates), and the panel hasn't reconnected yet. **Panel Moved Physically** The panel was moved to a different location or subnet but the network configuration wasn't updated. **Firmware Not Installed** New panels sometimes don't have firmware pre-installed and cannot communicate on the network until updated. --- ## Solutions ### Option 1: Power Cycle the Panel (Most Common Fix) A simple restart resolves most connectivity issues. **Steps:** 1. **Locate the power supply** connected to your panel 2. **Unplug the power cable** from the wall outlet or UPS 3. **Wait 30 seconds** (this ensures a full power drain) 4. **Plug the power back in** and wait for startup (typically 10-30 seconds) 5. **Check the LED status light** on the front of the panel (should be solid or slowly blinking green) 6. **Try to locate the panel again** in your software **What to expect:** The panel will display a startup sequence. Once fully booted, the network connectivity should restore automatically. --- ### Option 2: Test Panel Communication & Update Firmware if Needed If a power cycle didn't work, test whether the panel can communicate with your software. **Steps:** 1. **In your access control software**, go to **Device Configuration** or **Panel Setup** 2. **Right-click on the panel** and select **Test** or **Verify Connection** 3. **If the test passes**, the firmware is fine—move to Option 3 4. **If the test fails**, the panel cannot communicate and likely needs a firmware update: - Connect the panel directly to your computer using an Ethernet cable (bypassing your network if possible) - Right-click the panel and select **Firmware Update** - Click **OK** and allow 3-5 minutes for the update to complete - Once complete, the panel should restart and reconnect to your network **What to expect:** A successful test means the panel can communicate. If firmware update was needed, the panel may go offline briefly during the update, then automatically reconnect. --- ### Option 3: Verify Physical Connections & Basic Network Setup Check the hardware and basic network configuration. **Steps:** 1. **Verify the Ethernet cable is connected:** - Confirm the cable is fully inserted into both the panel's network port and your computer/switch - Look for a solid or blinking green light on the Ethernet port - Try a different Ethernet cable if one is available 2. **Check if your computer can see the panel's IP:** - In your software's Device Configuration, look for the panel's IP address - Try to ping that address from Command Prompt: type `ping [panel-ip-address]` - If ping works, the panel is reachable on your network 3. **For AC-825 panels**, check Windows Firewall settings: - Go to Control Panel > Windows Defender Firewall > Advanced Settings - Confirm that `AxtraxService.exe` has an inbound rule allowing connections - If no rule exists, create one: allow all connections for `AxtraxService.exe` 4. **If nothing above worked**, the panel may be on a different network than your computer: - This happens if the panel was moved to a different location or your IT department changed the network - Contact your IT department to verify both the panel and computer are on the same network segment - They may need to assign the panel a static IP address --- ### Option 4: Contact Your IT Department If the above steps didn't work, this is likely a network issue that requires IT support. **Information to provide to IT:** - **Panel IP address** (from your software's Device Configuration) - **Panel model number** (e.g., AC-215, AC-425, AC-825IP) - **What changed recently** (IT infrastructure updates, network changes, panel physically moved, etc.) - **Current status:** "My panel won't connect to the software" **Common issues IT can resolve:** - Panel is on a different network subnet than your computer - Network firewall rules are blocking communication - Panel needs a static IP address assignment - Network topology has changed since the panel was installed --- ## Network Specifications (Reference) For planning and troubleshooting purposes, here are the network specifications for Rosslare panels: | Connection Type | Maximum Distance | Cable Type | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **Ethernet (TCP/IP)** | 330 feet | Cat5e or Cat6 | Recommended for new installations | | **RS-485** | 2,000 feet | 22 AWG 120 Ω STP | For older panel networks | **System Scale:** AxTraxNG supports up to 1,024 panels across multiple networks (32 networks × 32 panels each). --- ## Prevention - **Update firmware regularly:** New firmware includes network stability improvements and security patches - **Use quality Ethernet cables:** Ensure cables are CAT5e or higher and fully inserted on both ends - **Use static IP addresses:** Configure panels with static IPs to prevent conflicts and simplify troubleshooting - **Maintain network documentation:** Keep a record of each panel's IP address, location, and hardware type - **Use a backup power supply:** Connect panels to an uninterruptible power supply (UPS) to avoid sudden power loss during updates - **Enable Windows Firewall:** AxTraxNG/Pro requires Windows Firewall to be enabled and properly configured - **Test connectivity regularly:** Periodically verify all panels are reachable and responding - **Restart routinely:** If issues recur periodically, schedule monthly restarts during maintenance windows --- ## Still Having Issues? If none of these steps resolve your issue: - **Note the panel model number** and IP address (if visible) - **Check the firmware version** shown in the software - **Document any error messages** displayed - **Note when the issue started** and what changed (power outage, network changes, recent updates) - **Contact support** with this information at [support contact info] --- ## Related Articles - [How to Update Panel Firmware](KB_02_Firmware_Updates.md) - [Troubleshooting: Panel Shows Offline in Software](advanced-troubleshooting.md) - [Network Setup Guide for Access Control Panels](network-setup.md) - [IP Address Configuration for AC-225 Panels](ac225-ip-setup.md) --- ## Revision History | Date | Change | |------|--------| | May 1, 2026 | Subject matter expert review: Reordered solutions by effectiveness (power cycle first), simplified network troubleshooting steps, removed overly technical ARP/UDP verification, made Windows Firewall AC-825 specific, simplified firmware update to use panel test method | | Apr 30, 2026 | Initial publication based on 64 support tickets |
